questions.question: Prove that for any positive integer $n$, the expression $n^3 - n$ is always divisible by $6$.
questions.answer: Since $n^3 - n = (n-1)n(n+1)$ and the product of three consecutive integers is always divisible by $3! = 6$, the expression $n^3 - n$ is divisible by $6$.

steps: Apply the algebraic identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$ to rewrite the expression as $(n-1)n(n+1)$.
Observe that $(n-1)$, $n$, and $(n+1)$ are three consecutive integers.
Identify the property that in any set of three consecutive integers, at least one must be a multiple of $2$ and exactly one must be a multiple of $3$.
Conclude that the product of three consecutive integers must be divisible by $2 \times 3 = 6$.
solution.derivation: $n^3 - n = n(n^2 - 1) \\ = (n-1)n(n+1) \\ \text{The product of } k \text{ consecutive integers is divisible by } k! \\ \text{For } k = 3, \text{ the product is divisible by } 3! = 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6 \\ \therefore (n-1)n(n+1) \text{ is divisible by } 6 \text{ for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$
